98: GQ2.tateDualityAt (G : Type) [Group G] [TopologicalSpace G] [IsTopologicalGroup G] (n : ‚Ąē) [NeZero n] [DistribMulAction G (GQ2.MuN n)] [ContinuousSMul G (GQ2.MuN n)] (hloc : GQ2.IsLocalDualizingGroup G n) : GQ2.TateDualityG G n**The B6 axiom (base-generalized to all finite `k/‚Ąö‚āā`).** Local Tate duality at any local Galois group `G` over `‚Ąö‚āā` (`G_‚Ąö‚āā` or a finite-index subgroup `G_K`, `K/‚Ąö‚āā` finite ‚ÄĒ the `IsLocalDualizingGroup` hypothesis): an invariant map `inv : H¬≤(G, őľ‚āô) ‚ČÉ+ ‚Ą§/n` making the evaluation cup pairings `H‚ĀĪ(G, Hom(M, őľ‚āô)) √ó H^{2‚ąíi}(G, M) ‚Üí H¬≤(G, őľ‚āô) ‚ČÖ ‚Ą§/n` perfect for every finite discrete `n`-torsion `G`-module `M`, in the three degree pairs `(0,2)`, `(1,1)`, `(2,0)`. Base-generalized 2026-07-06 (census-neutral, the B9/B11 pattern): NSW (7.2.6) already states Tate duality for arbitrary `p`-adic `k`, so the old `‚Ąö‚āā`-only form under-used its citation. The base member `k = ‚Ąö‚āā` is the in-repo `def GQ2.tateDuality` below (identity embedding), so every existing `G_‚Ąö‚āā` consumer is unchanged and `#print axioms` traces show `tateDualityAt`. Citation: **NSW [1], Ch. VII ¬ß7.2, Theorem (7.2.6)** (local Tate duality, for any `p`-adic `k`); Serre, *Galois Cohomology* II ¬ß5.2, Theorem 2; Milne, *ADT* I.2.3. Induced mod-2 Hilbert-pairing nondegeneracy (the P-15f7 consumer at `G_K`): FV Ch. IV ¬ß5 Prop (5.1)(6)/Cor./Thm (5.2), O'Meara ITQF 63:13. Paper: ¬ß¬ß5‚Äď8 (the `ūĚĒĹ‚āā` dimension counts), ¬ß6.3 (P-15f7); `docs/literature-axioms.md` B6, `docs/p15f7-axiom-proposal.md`.

See the module docstring for the faithfulness deviation (no `ŌÄ‚āĀ`) and the pinning of `őĻ`. Conjugation convention `x ^ c = c‚ĀĽ¬Ļ x c` (`GQ2.conjP`, matching `GQ2/Words.lean`).
96: GQ2.peripheralCyclotomicAction : GQ2.PeripheralCyclotomicAction**[Composite ‚ÄĒ B8.]** Local cyclotomic action on the peripheral inertia generators of `őĒ = ŌÄ‚āĀ^{pro-2}(‚Ąô¬Ļ ‚ąĖ {0,1,‚ąě})`: for every `u ‚ąą ‚Ą§‚āāň£` there is a continuous automorphism `ŌÜ_u` of `őĒ` sending each peripheral generator to a cyclotomic conjugate, `ŌÜ_u(P) = c_P‚ĀĽ¬Ļ ¬∑ P^u ¬∑ c_P` (and likewise `T`, `C`), the `u`-th power via ŠļĎ-exponentiation. This is Lemma 3.6's group-theoretic conclusion; see `GQ2/PeripheralAction.lean` for the deviation from the literal `ŌÄ‚āĀ` statement. **This is a composite leaf, not Stix alone** (adversarial review 2026-07-04, `docs/adversarial-axioms-review.md` ¬ß1). Stix supports that the decomposition group acts on cuspidal inertia *through the cyclotomic character*; producing an automorphism for **every** `u ‚ąą ‚Ą§‚āāň£` ‚ÄĒ the `aut : ‚Ą§_[2]ň£ ‚Üí ContinuousMulEquiv őĒ őĒ` field, quantified over all units ‚ÄĒ additionally needs a **cyclotomic-surjectivity** input (a decomposition-group element realizing each `u`). That input is carried in the census by **B5**'s `Ōá_cyc(rec u) = u‚ĀĽ¬Ļ` with dense reciprocity image (the machinery P-07 already exercises ‚ÄĒ `units_gen`/`markedHom_bijective`); a standalone global form (**B2** `cyclotomicCharacter_two_surjective`) was retained as citation companion until 2026-07-09, then deleted as unused (census decision, B12 board). The alternative of *weakening* B8 to quantify only over `u` in the cyclotomic image ‚ÄĒ deferring the surjectivity choice to each call site ‚ÄĒ was considered and **declined** (the downstream rewrite churn outweighs the ledger gain); B8 keeps the all-units form and this note carries the dependency. Citation: **Stix [8], ¬ß3.3 + Definition 37** (cuspidal inertia acts through the cyclotomic character ‚ÄĒ the paper's exact citation) **+ cyclotomic surjectivity (B5 locally; the global companion B2 was deleted 2026-07-09)**; classical origin Deligne, MSRI 16 (1989). Paper: Lemma 3.6. `docs/literature-axioms.md` B8.

844: GQ2.FiniteGroup.coprime_fiber_product.{u_1, u_2, u_3} {A : Type u_1} {B : Type u_2} {C : Type u_3} [Group A] [Group B] [Group C] [Finite A] [Finite B] (f : A ‚Üí* C) (g : B ‚Üí* C) (hf : Function.Surjective ‚áĎf) (hg : Function.Surjective ‚áĎg) (hcop : (Nat.card ‚Ü•f.ker).Coprime (Nat.card ‚Ü•g.ker)) (J : Subgroup (A √ó B)) (hJsub : ‚ąÄ p ‚ąą J, f p.1 = g p.2) (hJA : Function.Surjective fun p => (‚ÜĎp).1) (hJB : Function.Surjective fun p => (‚ÜĎp).2) (p : A √ó B) : f p.1 = g p.2 ‚Üí p ‚ąą J**Lemma 9.1 (coprime-kernel subdirect product).** Let `f : A ‚Ü† C` and `g : B ‚Ü† C` be finite epimorphisms whose kernels have coprime orders. A subgroup `J` of `A √ó B` that lies in the fibre product `{(a,b) | f a = g b}` and projects onto both factors is the *entire* fibre product. Proof via Goursat: `J.goursatFst ‚Č§ ker f` and `J.goursatSnd ‚Č§ ker g`, and Goursat's isomorphism `A/goursatFst ‚ČÉ B/goursatSnd` gives `|goursatFst|¬∑|ker g| = |goursatSnd|¬∑|ker f|`. Coprimality of `|ker f|`, `|ker g|` then forces `ker g ‚Č§ J.goursatSnd`, which is exactly the missing wild direction needed to hit every fibre-product element.

7: GQ2.OrientedTameQuotient (R : GQ2.LocalReciprocity) : Type**B10‚Ä≤ (oriented tame quotient), the bundle.** A B10 tame-quotient datum whose unramified coordinate `őĹ_t ‚ąė equiv ‚ąė mk` is *compatible with local reciprocity* (a bundle `R`, pinned to the B5 axiom at the `axiom` use-site): units land in the `őĹ_t`-kernel, and the uniformizer ‚ÄĒ `rec(2)` = *arithmetic* Frobenius ‚ÄĒ lands in the geometric-Frobenius coordinate `ztwoOne‚ĀĽ¬Ļ` (the repo's `tameSigma` is *geometric* Frobenius: `tame_relation` reads `ŌÉ‚ĀĽ¬ĻŌĄŌÉ = ŌĄ¬≤`, so `ŌÉ` is NSW (7.5.3)'s `ŌÉ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ`). Both clauses read the value through an arbitrary lift `g` of the abelianized class (well-posed: `őĹ_t ‚ąė equiv ‚ąė mk` kills `commClosure` ‚ÄĒ continuous into an abelian `T2` target). Citation: **Neukirch, *Algebraic Number Theory* (Grundlehren 322), Chap. V, Theorem (6.2)** ‚ÄĒ the norm residue symbol maps `U_K^{(n)}` onto the upper-numbering ramification group `G^n(L|K)` for every finite abelian `L|K`; at `n = 0`: **units ‚Ü¶ inertia**, whence prime elements ‚Ü¶ Frobenius lifts (with **Chap. V, (1.2)**: `ń§^i(G(L|K), U_L) = 1` for `L|K` unramified ‚ÄĒ units are norms at every finite unramified level; NSW [1] (7.1.2)(i) is the same statement in cohomological-triviality form). Tame structure and orientation: **NSW [1] (7.5.2)/(7.5.3)**. (All verified against the PDFs in `references/`.)

674: Prod.{u, v} (őĪ : Type u) (ő≤ : Type v) : Type (max u v)The product type, usually written `őĪ √ó ő≤`. Product types are also called pair or tuple types. Elements of this type are pairs in which the first element is an `őĪ` and the second element is a `ő≤`. Products nest to the right, so `(x, y, z) : őĪ √ó ő≤ √ó ő≥` is equivalent to `(x, (y, z)) : őĪ √ó (ő≤ √ó ő≥)`. Conventions for notations in identifiers: * The recommended spelling of `√ó` in identifiers is `Prod`.

66: GQ2.dyadicOrientation [CompactSpace GQ2.AbsGalQ2] [TotallyDisconnectedSpace GQ2.AbsGalQ2] : GQ2.DyadicOrientation**The B3c axiom** (composite interface ‚ÄĒ Labute [2], Th√©or√®me 4, case (2): `q = 2`, `n = 3` odd, `f = 2`). There is a B4 isomorphism `Ōą : G_{‚Ąö‚āā}(2) ‚ČÖ D‚āÄ` and a continuous descent `Ōá‚āā` of the cyclotomic character through `G_{‚Ąö‚āā} ‚Ü† G_{‚Ąö‚āā}(2)`, surjective (image invariant `{¬Ī1} √ó U‚āā‚ĀĹ¬≤‚Āĺ = ‚Ą§‚āāň£`), with values `(Ōá(A), Ōá(S), Ōá(Y)) = (‚ąí1, 1, (‚ąí3)‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)` ‚ÄĒ the paper's `Ōá_D`-row of eq. (13) (Lemmas 3.4/3.5). **Composite classification** (adversarial review 2026-07-04, `docs/adversarial-axioms-review.md` ¬ß3): this is **not** a bare Labute citation. It bundles (a) Labute's orientation/classification values, (b) the local-Galois fact that the Demushkin dualizing character equals the cyclotomic character (through *this* quotient map ‚ÄĒ Labute Thm 4 does not by itself assert `chiCyc`-compatibility), and (c) the choice of a **normalized** B4 isomorphism realizing (a)+(b) on the marked generators. Consequently **B3c subsumes a marked version of B4**: a downstream declaration whose `#print axioms` shows `dyadicOrientation` need not *also* list B4 in its **Ax** column unless B4 is consumed independently (the review-packet classification table, `docs/review-packet.md` ¬ß2, records this). Deviation (route (ii), flagged in `GQ2/Orientation.lean`): the *abstract* dualizing characterization of the canonical character (Labute Prop. 6) is not formalized; the bundle asserts exactly the interface the paper consumes. Citation: Labute [2], Th√©or√®me 4 case (2) and Th√©or√®me 8 (Canad. J. Math. 19 (1967), 106‚Äď132); dualizing character = cyclotomic through this quotient: NSW [1], Ch. VII ¬ß7.5, (7.5.11)‚Äď(7.5.12); Serre [3]. Paper: Lemma 3.4 ‚Üí Prop. 1.1. `docs/literature-axioms.md` B3/B3c.

6: GQ2.tameQuotient : GQ2.OrientedTameQuotient GQ2.localReciprocity**[Classical ‚ÄĒ B10 (oriented form, B10‚Ä≤).]** The tame quotient of `G_‚Ąö‚āā`, *oriented against local reciprocity*: a closed normal pro-2 subgroup `W ‚Č§ G_‚Ąö‚āā` (wild inertia) with `G_‚Ąö‚āā/W ‚ČÖ T_tame = ‚ü®ŌÉ, ŌĄ ‚ą£ ŌĄ^ŌÉ = ŌĄ¬≤‚ü©_prof`, whose unramified coordinate `őĹ_t` matches B5's reciprocity normalization ‚ÄĒ `őĹ_t(tameF(rec u)) = 1` for units `u` and `őĹ_t(tameF(rec 2)) = ztwoOne‚ĀĽ¬Ļ` (arithmetic Frobenius, geometric coordinate). Citation, existence: **NSW [1], Ch. VII ¬ß7.5, Theorem (7.5.3) (Iwasawa)** ‚ÄĒ `G(k_tr|k)` is the profinite group on `ŌÉ, ŌĄ` with the single relation `ŌÉŌĄŌÉ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ = ŌĄ^q` (`q = 2`); with **(7.5.2)** (split extension `1 ‚Üí Šļź^{(p‚Ä≤)}(1) ‚Üí G(k_tr|k) ‚Üí őď ‚Üí 1`) and `G(kŐĄ|k_tr)` pro-`p` (Serre, *Local Fields* [7], Ch. IV). Citation, orientation clauses: **Neukirch, *Algebraic Number Theory* (Grundlehren 322), Chap. V, Theorem (6.2)** (norm residue symbol maps `U_K^{(n)}` onto the upper-numbering ramification groups; `n = 0`: units ‚Ü¶ inertia, so prime elements ‚Ü¶ Frobenius lifts) with **Chap. V, (1.2)** / NSW [1] (7.1.2)(i) (units are norms in unramified extensions). (All verified against the PDFs in `references/`; the Frobenius-direction convention `ŌÉ = geometric` and the clause encoding are documented at `OrientedTameQuotient` in `GQ2/TameQuotient.lean`.) History: added census 10‚Üí11 as the unoriented `TameQuotientData` (P-06 escalation); **strengthened in place to the oriented form (census unchanged) 2026-07-06** (P-25 escalation, user-approved) ‚ÄĒ the orientation discharges `tame_reciprocity` (`docs/p25-tame-reciprocity-plan.md`), whose derivation from B5's `norm_reciprocity` alone is blocked by the absence of local ramification theory for `Field.absoluteGaloisGroup` in Mathlib. Paper: Prop. 3.2 local side + Prop. 3.14 / Cor. 3.12 (the "same natural unramified character"). `docs/literature-axioms.md` B10.

56: Int : TypeThe integers. This type is special-cased by the compiler and overridden with an efficient implementation. 54: GQ2.HilbertSymbol.hilbertSymbol_dyadic (őĪ ő≤ : ‚Ą§) (u v : ‚Ą§_[2]ň£) : GQ2.HilbertSymbol.hilbertSymbol (GQ2.HilbertSymbol.unit2 ^ őĪ * GQ2.HilbertSymbol.unitCoe u) (GQ2.HilbertSymbol.unit2 ^ ő≤ * GQ2.HilbertSymbol.unitCoe v) = GQ2.HilbertSymbol.signOf (GQ2.HilbertSymbol.őĶ u * GQ2.HilbertSymbol.őĶ v + ‚ÜĎőĪ * GQ2.HilbertSymbol.ŌČ v + ‚ÜĎő≤ * GQ2.HilbertSymbol.ŌČ u)**B7‚Ä≤ (dyadic Hilbert symbol), `[Classical.]` ‚ÄĒ discharged 2026-07-09 (B7‚Ä≤ board).** Writing `a = 2^őĪ u`, `b = 2^ő≤ v` with `u, v ‚ąą ‚Ą§‚āāň£`, the Hilbert symbol over `‚Ąö‚āā` is `(a, b)‚āā = (-1)^{őĶ(u) őĶ(v) + őĪ ŌČ(v) + ő≤ ŌČ(u)}`. Convention: `signOf` sends the `ūĚĒĹ‚āā`-valued exponent to `{¬Ī1} = ‚Ą§ň£`; every element of `‚Ąö‚āāň£` has the form `2^őĪ u` (`őĪ ‚ąą ‚Ą§`, `u ‚ąą ‚Ą§‚āāň£`), so this determines the symbol on all of `‚Ąö‚āāň£ √ó ‚Ąö‚āāň£`. Formerly the axiom **B7‚Ä≤**; since 2026-07-09 a same-name theorem over the std-3 proof `hilbertSymbol_dyadic'` (`GQ2/HilbertSymbolDyadicClose.lean` ‚ÄĒ 2-adic Hensel, the norm-form `(a,b) = (a,‚ąíab)` identity, and finite mod-8 `decide`s; zero consumer churn, the B11/B12 pattern).

536: GQ2.GaussSigns.prop_6_9_unramified_of_cyclic.{u_1, u_2} {V : Type u_1} [AddCommGroup V] [Finite V] (q : V ‚Üí ZMod 2) (hq : GQ2.QuadraticFp2.IsQuadraticFp2 q) (hns : GQ2.QuadraticFp2.Nonsingular q) (m : ‚Ąē) (hm : 1 ‚Č§ m) (hcard : Nat.card V = 2 ^ (2 * m)) (h2 : ‚ąÄ (v : V), v + v = 0) {Hf : Type u_2} [Group Hf] [Finite Hf] [DistribMulAction Hf V] (g : Hf) (hgen : ‚ąÄ (x : Hf), x ‚ąą Subgroup.zpowers g) (hfaith : ‚ąÄ (h : Hf), (‚ąÄ (v : V), h ‚ÄĘ v = v) ‚Üí h = 1) (hsimple : ‚ąÄ (W : AddSubgroup V), (‚ąÄ (h : Hf), ‚ąÄ w ‚ąą W, h ‚ÄĘ w ‚ąą W) ‚Üí W = ‚ä• ‚ą® W = ‚ä§) (hinv : ‚ąÄ (h : Hf) (v : V), q (h ‚ÄĘ v) = q v) : GQ2.QuadraticFp2.zeroCount q = 2 ^ (2 * m - 1) - 2 ^ (m - 1)**Proposition 6.9, unramified case, from a cyclic generator** ‚ÄĒ the complete unramified reduction. If `Hf` is generated by a single `g` (the Frobenius) acting on the exponent-2 space `V` (`#V = 2^(2m)`) faithfully, simply, preserving a nonsingular `q`, then `#q‚ĀĽ¬Ļ(0) = 2^(2m‚ąí1) ‚ąí 2^(m‚ąí1)`. Both hypotheses of `prop_6_9_unramified_of_abelian` are discharged here: abelianness is immediate from cyclicity, and the arithmetic input `#Hf ‚ą§ 2^m ‚ąí 1` comes from the operator crux `irreducible_operator_pow_ne_one` applied to `T = (g ‚ÄĘ ¬∑)` (were `#Hf ‚ą£ 2^m ‚ąí 1` we would have `T^(2^m‚ąí1) = 1` for the irreducible `T` on the `2m`-dimensional `V`, which it forbids).

If a finite **abelian** group `Hf` acts on `V` (`#V = 2^(2m)`) faithfully, simply, preserving a nonsingular `q`, with `#Hf ‚ą§ 2^m ‚ąí 1`, then `#q‚ĀĽ¬Ļ(0) = 2^(2m‚ąí1) ‚ąí 2^(m‚ąí1)`. The action is automatically free on `V ‚ąĖ 0`: for `g ‚Č† 1`, the fixed space `{v | g ‚ÄĘ v = v}` is `Hf`-stable (by commutativity), so `‚ä•` or `‚ä§` by simplicity, and `‚ä§` would make `g` act trivially (contradicting faithfulness). This is exactly the unramified geometry ‚ÄĒ `Hf` is the cyclic Frobenius image ‚ÄĒ modulo the arithmetic input `#Hf ‚ą§ 2^m ‚ąí 1` (equivalently: the generator is not contained in the proper subfield `ūĚĒĹ_{2^m}`, i.e. `V` is genuinely `2m`-dimensional and simple).

523: Not (a : Prop) : Prop`Not p`, or `¬¨p`, is the negation of `p`. It is defined to be `p ‚Üí False`, so if your goal is `¬¨p` you can use `intro h` to turn the goal into `h : p ‚äĘ False`, and if you have `hn : ¬¨p` and `h : p` then `hn h : False` and `(hn h).elim` will prove anything. For more information: [Propositional Logic](https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/propositions_and_proofs.html#propositional-logic) Conventions for notations in identifiers: * The recommended spelling of `¬¨` in identifiers is `not`.
521: MulAction.{u_9, u_10} (őĪ : Type u_9) (ő≤ : Type u_10) [Monoid őĪ] : Type (max u_10 u_9)Type class for monoid actions on types, with notation `g ‚ÄĘ p`. The `MulAction G P` typeclass says that the monoid `G` acts multiplicatively on a type `P`. More precisely this means that the action satisfies the two axioms `1 ‚ÄĘ p = p` and `(g‚āĀ * g‚āā) ‚ÄĘ p = g‚āĀ ‚ÄĘ (g‚āā ‚ÄĘ p)`. A mathematician might simply say that the monoid `G` acts on `P`. For example, if `G` is a group and `X` is a type, if a mathematician says say "let `G` act on the set `X`" they will probably mean `[MulAction G X]`. [Wikidata Q288465](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q288465)

49: And (a b : Prop) : Prop`And a b`, or `a ‚ąß b`, is the conjunction of propositions. Conventions for notations in identifiers: * The recommended spelling of `‚ąß` in identifiers is `and`.

47: Finite.{u_3} (őĪ : Sort u_3) : PropA type is `Finite` if it is in bijective correspondence to some `Fin n`. This is similar to `Fintype`, but `Finite` is a proposition rather than data. A particular benefit to this is that `Finite` instances are definitionally equal to one another (due to proof irrelevance) rather than being merely propositionally equal, and, furthermore, `Finite` instances generally avoid the need for `Decidable` instances. One other notable difference is that `Finite` allows there to be `Finite p` instances for all `p : Prop`, which is not allowed by `Fintype` due to universe constraints. An application of this is that `Finite (x ‚ąą s ‚Üí ő≤ x)` follows from the general instance for pi types, assuming `[‚ąÄ x, Finite (ő≤ x)]`. Implementation note: this is a reason `Finite őĪ` is not defined as `Nonempty (Fintype őĪ)`. Every `Fintype` instance provides a `Finite` instance via `Finite.of_fintype`. Conversely, one can noncomputably create a `Fintype` instance from a `Finite` instance via `Fintype.ofFinite`. In a proof one might write ```lean have := Fintype.ofFinite őĪ ``` to obtain such an instance. Do not write noncomputable `Fintype` instances; instead write `Finite` instances and use this `Fintype.ofFinite` interface. The `Fintype` instances should be relied upon to be computable for evaluation purposes. Theorems should use `Finite` instead of `Fintype`, unless definitions in the theorem statement require `Fintype`. Definitions should prefer `Finite` as well, unless it is important that the definitions are meant to be computable in the reduction or `#eval` sense.

464: Fintype.card.{u_4} (őĪ : Type u_4) [Fintype őĪ] : ‚Ąē`card őĪ` is the number of elements in `őĪ`, defined when `őĪ` is a fintype.
461: Fintype.{u_4} (őĪ : Type u_4) : Type u_4`Fintype őĪ` means that `őĪ` is finite, i.e. there are only finitely many distinct elements of type `őĪ`. The evidence of this is a finset `elems` (a list up to permutation without duplicates), together with a proof that everything of type `őĪ` is in the list.
460: Field.{u} (K : Type u) : Type uA `Field` is a `CommRing` with multiplicative inverses for nonzero elements. An instance of `Field K` includes maps `ratCast : ‚Ąö ‚Üí K` and `qsmul : ‚Ąö ‚Üí K ‚Üí K`. Those two fields are needed to implement the `DivisionRing K ‚Üí Algebra ‚Ąö K` instance since we need to control the specific definitions for some special cases of `K` (in particular `K = ‚Ąö` itself). See also note [forgetful inheritance]. If the field has positive characteristic `p`, our division by zero convention forces `ratCast (1 / p) = 1 / 0 = 0`.
46: ContinuousSMul.{u_1, u_2} (M : Type u_1) (X : Type u_2) [SMul M X] [TopologicalSpace M] [TopologicalSpace X] : PropClass `ContinuousSMul M X` says that the scalar multiplication `(‚ÄĘ) : M ‚Üí X ‚Üí X` is continuous in both arguments. We use the same class for all kinds of multiplicative actions, including (semi)modules and algebras.
459: GQ2.DeepPart.hermitian_form_eq_trace_form.{u_2} {D : Type u_2} [Field D] [Fintype D] {m : ‚Ąē} (hm : 1 ‚Č§ m) (hcard : Fintype.card D = 2 ^ (2 * m)) [Algebra (ZMod (ringChar D)) D] (e2 : ZMod (ringChar D) ‚ČÉ+ ZMod 2) (Q : D ‚Üí ZMod 2) (hQ : GQ2.QuadraticFp2.IsQuadraticFp2 Q) (hns : GQ2.QuadraticFp2.Nonsingular Q) (hU : ‚ąÄ (u : Dň£), u ^ (2 ^ m + 1) = 1 ‚Üí ‚ąÄ (x : D), Q (‚ÜĎu * x) = Q x) : ‚ąÉ c, c ^ 2 ^ m ‚Č† c ‚ąß ‚ąÄ (x : D), Q x = e2 ((Algebra.trace (ZMod (ringChar D)) D) (c * x ^ (2 ^ m + 1)))**Lemma 6.7 (invariant quadratic forms on a Hermitian line), existence form**: every nonsingular quadratic form on `D = ūĚĒĹ_{2^{2m}}` invariant under the norm-one circle `U = {u : u^{2^m+1} = 1}` is the Hermitian trace form of some `c` outside the fixed field. (The adjoint identity holds on a subring containing `U`, hence everywhere; the polar form is then trace-represented with Frobenius-fixed coefficient, an Artin‚ÄďSchreier preimage matches the polars, and the additive `U`-invariant difference vanishes.)

(86)**: for a nonsingular `q` and an orthogonal operator `U` of 2-power order, the doubling `q_U(x) = q(x) + B(x, Ux)` is nonsingular and `Arf(q_U) = Arf(q) + rank(1 + U) (mod 2)`. 454: id.{u} {őĪ : Sort u} (a : őĪ) : őĪThe identity function. `id` takes an implicit argument `őĪ : Sort u` (a type in any universe), and an argument `a : őĪ`, and returns `a`. Although this may look like a useless function, one application of the identity function is to explicitly put a type on an expression. If `e` has type `T`, and `T'` is definitionally equal to `T`, then `@id T' e` typechecks, and Lean knows that this expression has type `T'` rather than `T`. This can make a difference for typeclass inference, since `T` and `T'` may have different typeclass instances on them. `show T' from e` is sugar for an `@id T' e` expression.

44: DistribMulAction.{u_12, u_13} (M : Type u_12) (A : Type u_13) [Monoid M] [AddMonoid A] : Type (max u_12 u_13)Typeclass for multiplicative actions on additive structures. For example, if `G` is a group (with group law written as multiplication) and `A` is an abelian group (with group law written as addition), then to give `A` a `G`-module structure (for example, to use the theory of group cohomology) is to say `[DistribMulAction G A]`. Note in that we do not use the `Module` typeclass for `G`-modules, as the `Module` typeclass is for modules over a ring rather than a group. Mathematically, `DistribMulAction G A` is equivalent to giving `A` the structure of a `‚Ą§[G]`-module.

419: GQ2.FoxH.selfDual_of_ramified.{u_1, u_2} {C : Type u_1} [Group C] [Finite C] {A : Type u_2} [AddCommGroup A] [Finite A] [DistribMulAction C A] (t : GQ2.Marking C) (ht : t.TameRel) (hw : t.WildRel) (hgen : t.Generates) (hV‚āā : ‚ąÄ (v : A), v + v = 0) (hsimple : GQ2.FoxH.IsSimpleModTwo C A) (hcore : t.Pro2Core) (htau : ‚ąÄ (v : A), t.ŌĄ ‚ÄĘ v = v ‚Üí v = 0) : GQ2.FoxH.IsSelfDual t A**Proposition 5.15, ramified simple case.** A simple module with `V^T = 0` is self-dual. `hTodd` (ŌĄ odd-order) is derived (`tau_powOmega2_smul_trivial`); the dual `A‚ą®` inherits wild-triviality and `hTodd` (contragredient) and ŌĄ-fixed-point-freeness (`(ŌĄ‚ĀĽ¬Ļ‚ąí1)` surjective); the pairing `őĽ((1+U+U‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)c)` (`lemma_5_13_pairing_ramified`) is perfect because the operator `1+U+U‚ĀĽ¬Ļ` is unipotent, hence bijective (`sigma2_pairing_operator_injective`) ‚ÄĒ no ŌÉ-tameness `hU` anywhere (it is *not derivable*: `S‚āÉ`/`C‚āÖ‚čäC‚āĄ` admissible counterexamples).
414: GQ2.FoxH.selfDual_of_split.{u_1, u_2} {C : Type u_1} [Group C] [Finite C] {A : Type u_2} [AddCommGroup A] [Finite A] [DistribMulAction C A] (t : GQ2.Marking C) (ht : t.TameRel) (hw : t.WildRel) (hgen : t.Generates) (hV‚āā : ‚ąÄ (v : A), v + v = 0) (hsimple : GQ2.FoxH.IsSimpleModTwo C A) (hcore : t.Pro2Core) (htau : ‚ąÄ (v : A), t.ŌĄ ‚ÄĘ v = v) (hŌÉ : ‚ąÉ v, t.ŌÉ ‚ÄĘ v ‚Č† v) : GQ2.FoxH.IsSelfDual t A**Proposition 5.15, split simple case.** A nontrivial simple module on which `ŌĄ` acts trivially (`htau`) and `ŌÉ` acts nontrivially (`hŌÉ`) is self-dual. 406: GQ2.FoxH.lemma_5_13_pairing_ramified.{u_1, u_2} {C : Type u_1} [Group C] [Finite C] {V : Type u_2} [AddCommGroup V] [DistribMulAction C V] (t : GQ2.Marking C) (ht : t.TameRel) (hw : t.WildRel) (hV‚āā : ‚ąÄ (v : V), v + v = 0) [Finite V] (hx0 : ‚ąÄ (v : V), t.x‚āÄ ‚ÄĘ v = v) (hx1 : ‚ąÄ (v : V), t.x‚āĀ ‚ÄĘ v = v) (htau : ‚ąÄ (v : V), t.ŌĄ ‚ÄĘ v = v ‚Üí v = 0) (hTodd : ‚ąÄ (v : V), GQ2.powOmega2 t.ŌĄ ‚ÄĘ v = v) (c : V) (lam : GQ2.FoxH.ElemDual V) : GQ2.FoxH.mixedB t (GQ2.FoxH.x0Supported c) (GQ2.FoxH.x0Supported lam) = lam (c + t.sigma2 ‚ÄĘ c + t.sigma2‚ĀĽ¬Ļ ‚ÄĘ c)**Lemma 5.13, pairing display (54), ramified case**: when `V^T = 0` the pairing on `x‚āÄ`-supported representatives is `(c, őĽ) ‚Ü¶ őĽ((1 + U + U‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)c)` for `U = S‚āā^ŌČ` (`Marking.sigma2`). 4: Eq.{u_1} {őĪ : Sort u_1} : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí PropThe equality relation. It has one introduction rule, `Eq.refl`. We use `a = b` as notation for `Eq a b`. A fundamental property of equality is that it is an equivalence relation. ``` variable (őĪ : Type) (a b c d : őĪ) variable (hab : a = b) (hcb : c = b) (hcd : c = d) example : a = d := Eq.trans (Eq.trans hab (Eq.symm hcb)) hcd ``` Equality is much more than an equivalence relation, however. It has the important property that every assertion respects the equivalence, in the sense that we can substitute equal expressions without changing the truth value. That is, given `h1 : a = b` and `h2 : p a`, we can construct a proof for `p b` using substitution: `Eq.subst h1 h2`. Example: ``` example (őĪ : Type) (a b : őĪ) (p : őĪ ‚Üí Prop) (h1 : a = b) (h2 : p a) : p b := Eq.subst h1 h2 example (őĪ : Type) (a b : őĪ) (p : őĪ ‚Üí Prop) (h1 : a = b) (h2 : p a) : p b := h1 ‚Ėł h2 ``` The triangle in the second presentation is a macro built on top of `Eq.subst` and `Eq.symm`, and you can enter it by typing `\t`. For more information: [Equality](https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/quantifiers_and_equality.html#equality) Conventions for notations in identifiers: * The recommended spelling of `=` in identifiers is `eq`.

335: FreeGroup.{u} (őĪ : Type u) : Type uIf `őĪ` is a type, then `FreeGroup őĪ` is the free group generated by `őĪ`. This is a group equipped with a function `FreeGroup.of : őĪ ‚Üí FreeGroup őĪ` which has the following universal property: if `G` is any group, and `f : őĪ ‚Üí G` is any function, then this function is the composite of `FreeGroup.of` and a unique group homomorphism `FreeGroup.lift f : FreeGroup őĪ ‚Üí* G`. A typical element of `FreeGroup őĪ` is a formal product of elements of `őĪ` and their formal inverses, quotient by reduction. For example if `x` and `y` are terms of type `őĪ` then `x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ * y * y * x * y‚ĀĽ¬Ļ` is a "typical" element of `FreeGroup őĪ`. In particular if `őĪ` is empty then `FreeGroup őĪ` is isomorphic to the trivial group, and if `őĪ` has one term then `FreeGroup őĪ` is isomorphic to `Multiplicative ‚Ą§`. If `őĪ` has two or more terms then `FreeGroup őĪ` is not commutative. [Wikidata Q431078](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q431078)

30: GQ2.unramifiedQuadratic_units_are_norms (k : IntermediateField ‚Ąö_[2] (AlgebraicClosure ‚Ąö_[2])) [FiniteDimensional ‚Ąö_[2] ‚Ü•k] (a : (‚Ü•k)ň£) (őīa : AlgebraicClosure ‚Ąö_[2]) (hőīa : őīa ^ 2 = ‚ÜĎ‚ÜĎa) (hunram : GQ2.IsUnramifiedQuadraticSpectral k őīa) (u : (‚Ü•k)ň£) : ‚ÄĖ‚ÜĎ‚ÜĎu‚ÄĖ = 1 ‚Üí ‚ąÉ x y, ‚ÜĎu = x ^ 2 - ‚ÜĎa * y ^ 2**B11b (unramified unit-norm surjectivity) ‚ÄĒ DISCHARGED 2026-07-09: now a theorem.** If `k(‚ąöa)/k` is unramified (the `IsUnramifiedQuadraticSpectral` convention on a chosen root `őīa`, `őīa¬≤ = a`), then every unit of `k` (`‚ÄĖu‚ÄĖ = 1`) is a norm from `k(‚ąöa)` ‚ÄĒ i.e. `u = x¬≤ ‚ąí a y¬≤` is solvable in `k`. Paper: ¬ß6.3 (unramified-norm input to the local calculation). **Discharged** (B11b board, `docs/orchestration/b11b-tickets.md` / `b11b-proof-plan.md`, user-approved census flip 2026-07-09): proved **std-3** (no B-axioms) in `GQ2/UnramifiedQuadraticNorms.lean` (+ the ŌÉ-free `GQ2/TeichmullerLift.lean` bricks) ‚ÄĒ completing the square (the involution `ŌÉőī = ‚ąíőī` via the infinite Galois correspondence) and a depth-by-depth norm-form approximation `w‚āô‚āä‚āĀ = w‚āô(1 + ŌÄ‚ĀŅ‚Āļ¬Ļz‚āÄ)` against the B13 unit filtration, with the increment supplied by the exact trace coverage `z ‚Ü¶ z + ŌÉz`. 145: Subtype.{u} {őĪ : Sort u} (p : őĪ ‚Üí Prop) : Sort (max 1 u)All the elements of a type that satisfy a predicate. `Subtype p`, usually written `{ x : őĪ // p x }` or `{ x // p x }`, contains all elements `x : őĪ` for which `p x` is true. Its constructor is a pair of the value and the proof that it satisfies the predicate. In run-time code, `{ x : őĪ // p x }` is represented identically to `őĪ`. There is a coercion from `{ x : őĪ // p x }` to `őĪ`, so elements of a subtype may be used where the underlying type is expected. Examples: * `{ n : Nat // n % 2 = 0 }` is the type of even numbers. * `{ xs : Array String // xs.size = 5 }` is the type of arrays with five `String`s. * Given `xs : List őĪ`, `List { x : őĪ // x ‚ąą xs }` is the type of lists in which all elements are contained in `xs`. Conventions for notations in identifiers: * The recommended spelling of `{ x // p x }` in identifiers is `subtype`.

1000871: GQ2.unramifiedQuadratic_units_are_norms (k : IntermediateField ‚Ąö_[2] (AlgebraicClosure ‚Ąö_[2])) [FiniteDimensional ‚Ąö_[2] ‚Ü•k] (a : (‚Ü•k)ň£) (őīa : AlgebraicClosure ‚Ąö_[2]) (hőīa : őīa ^ 2 = ‚ÜĎ‚ÜĎa) (hunram : GQ2.IsUnramifiedQuadraticSpectral k őīa) (u : (‚Ü•k)ň£) : ‚ÄĖ‚ÜĎ‚ÜĎu‚ÄĖ = 1 ‚Üí ‚ąÉ x y, ‚ÜĎu = x ^ 2 - ‚ÜĎa * y ^ 2**B11b (unramified unit-norm surjectivity) ‚ÄĒ DISCHARGED 2026-07-09: now a theorem.** If `k(‚ąöa)/k` is unramified (the `IsUnramifiedQuadraticSpectral` convention on a chosen root `őīa`, `őīa¬≤ = a`), then every unit of `k` (`‚ÄĖu‚ÄĖ = 1`) is a norm from `k(‚ąöa)` ‚ÄĒ i.e. `u = x¬≤ ‚ąí a y¬≤` is solvable in `k`. 1000814: GQ2.dyadicOrientation [CompactSpace GQ2.AbsGalQ2] [TotallyDisconnectedSpace GQ2.AbsGalQ2] : GQ2.DyadicOrientation**The B3c axiom** (composite interface ‚ÄĒ Labute [2], Th√©or√®me 4, case (2): `q = 2`, `n = 3` odd, `f = 2`). There is a B4 isomorphism `Ōą : G_{‚Ąö‚āā}(2) ‚ČÖ D‚āÄ` and a continuous descent `Ōá‚āā` of the cyclotomic character through `G_{‚Ąö‚āā} ‚Ü† G_{‚Ąö‚āā}(2)`, surjective (image invariant `{¬Ī1} √ó U‚āā‚ĀĹ¬≤‚Āĺ = ‚Ą§‚āāň£`), with values `(Ōá(A), Ōá(S), Ōá(Y)) = (‚ąí1, 1, (‚ąí3)‚ĀĽ¬Ļ)` ‚ÄĒ the paper's `Ōá_D`-row of eq. (13) (Lemmas 3.4/3.5). **Composite classification** (adversarial review 2026-07-04, `docs/adversarial-axioms-review.md` ¬ß3): this is **not** a bare Labute citation. It bundles (a) Labute's orientation/classification values, (b) the local-Galois fact that the Demushkin dualizing character equals the cyclotomic character (through *this* quotient map ‚ÄĒ Labute Thm 4 does not by itself assert `chiCyc`-compatibility), and (c) the choice of a **normalized** B4 isomorphism realizing (a)+(b) on the marked generators. Consequently **B3c subsumes a marked version of B4**: a downstream declaration whose `#print axioms` shows `dyadicOrientation` need not *also* list B4 in its **Ax** column unless B4 is consumed independently (the review-packet classification table, `docs/review-packet.md` ¬ß2, records this). Deviation (route (ii), flagged in `GQ2/Orientation.lean`): the *abstract* dualizing characterization of the canonical character (Labute Prop. 6) is not formalized; the bundle asserts exactly the interface the paper consumes. Citation: Labute [2], Th√©or√®me 4 case (2) and Th√©or√®me 8 (Canad. J. Math. 19 (1967), 106‚Äď132); dualizing character = cyclotomic through this quotient: NSW [1], Ch. VII ¬ß7.5, (7.5.11)‚Äď(7.5.12); Serre [3]. Paper: Lemma 3.4 ‚Üí Prop. 1.1. `docs/literature-axioms.md` B3/B3c.

1000339: Int : TypeThe integers. This type is special-cased by the compiler and overridden with an efficient implementation. The runtime has a special representation for `Int` that stores ‚Äúsmall‚ÄĚ signed numbers directly, while larger numbers use a fast arbitrary-precision arithmetic library (usually [GMP](https://gmplib.org/)). A ‚Äúsmall number‚ÄĚ is an integer that can be encoded with one fewer bits than the platform's pointer size (i.e. 63 bits on 64-bit architectures and 31 bits on 32-bit architectures).

1000331: MulAction.{u_9, u_10} (őĪ : Type u_9) (ő≤ : Type u_10) [Monoid őĪ] : Type (max u_10 u_9)Type class for monoid actions on types, with notation `g ‚ÄĘ p`. The `MulAction G P` typeclass says that the monoid `G` acts multiplicatively on a type `P`. More precisely this means that the action satisfies the two axioms `1 ‚ÄĘ p = p` and `(g‚āĀ * g‚āā) ‚ÄĘ p = g‚āĀ ‚ÄĘ (g‚āā ‚ÄĘ p)`. A mathematician might simply say that the monoid `G` acts on `P`. For example, if `G` is a group and `X` is a type, if a mathematician says say "let `G` act on the set `X`" they will probably mean `[MulAction G X]`. [Wikidata Q288465](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q288465)

1000308: id.{u} {őĪ : Sort u} (a : őĪ) : őĪThe identity function. `id` takes an implicit argument `őĪ : Sort u` (a type in any universe), and an argument `a : őĪ`, and returns `a`. Although this may look like a useless function, one application of the identity function is to explicitly put a type on an expression. If `e` has type `T`, and `T'` is definitionally equal to `T`, then `@id T' e` typechecks, and Lean knows that this expression has type `T'` rather than `T`. This can make a difference for typeclass inference, since `T` and `T'` may have different typeclass instances on them. `show T' from e` is sugar for an `@id T' e` expression.

1000244: FreeGroup.{u} (őĪ : Type u) : Type uIf `őĪ` is a type, then `FreeGroup őĪ` is the free group generated by `őĪ`. This is a group equipped with a function `FreeGroup.of : őĪ ‚Üí FreeGroup őĪ` which has the following universal property: if `G` is any group, and `f : őĪ ‚Üí G` is any function, then this function is the composite of `FreeGroup.of` and a unique group homomorphism `FreeGroup.lift f : FreeGroup őĪ ‚Üí* G`. A typical element of `FreeGroup őĪ` is a formal product of elements of `őĪ` and their formal inverses, quotient by reduction. For example if `x` and `y` are terms of type `őĪ` then `x‚ĀĽ¬Ļ * y * y * x * y‚ĀĽ¬Ļ` is a "typical" element of `FreeGroup őĪ`. In particular if `őĪ` is empty then `FreeGroup őĪ` is isomorphic to the trivial group, and if `őĪ` has one term then `FreeGroup őĪ` is isomorphic to `Multiplicative ‚Ą§`. If `őĪ` has two or more terms then `FreeGroup őĪ` is not commutative. [Wikidata Q431078](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Q431078)

1000152: GQ2.FoxH.classTwoCore.{u_1} {G : Type u_1} [Group G] (A B : G) (hcentral : ‚ąÄ (z : G), GQ2.commP A B * z = z * GQ2.commP A B) (hk2 : GQ2.commP A B * GQ2.commP A B = 1) (hB4 : B ^ 4 = 1) : A * B * B ^ 2 * GQ2.commP A B = B‚ĀĽ¬Ļ * A**Lemma 5.2, core cancellation.** If the commutator `k = [A,B]` (`commP` convention `A‚ĀĽ¬ĻB‚ĀĽ¬ĻAB`) is central and satisfies `k¬≤ = 1`, and `B‚Āī = 1`, then `A¬∑B¬∑B¬≤¬∑[A,B] = B‚ĀĽ¬Ļ¬∑A`. 1000148: GQ2.Foundations.absGalQ2_localEulerCharacteristic.{u_1} (M : Type u_1) [AddCommGroup M] [TopologicalSpace M] [DiscreteTopology M] [DistribMulAction GQ2.AbsGalQ2 M] [ContinuousSMul GQ2.AbsGalQ2 M] [Finite M] : Finite ‚Ü•(GQ2.ContCoh.H0 GQ2.AbsGalQ2 M) ‚ąß Finite (GQ2.ContCoh.H1 GQ2.AbsGalQ2 M) ‚ąß Finite (GQ2.ContCoh.H2 GQ2.AbsGalQ2 M) ‚ąß Nat.card (GQ2.ContCoh.H1 GQ2.AbsGalQ2 M) = Nat.card ‚Ü•(GQ2.ContCoh.H0 GQ2.AbsGalQ2 M) * Nat.card (GQ2.ContCoh.H2 GQ2.AbsGalQ2 M) * 2 ^ padicValNat 2 (Nat.card M)**[Classical ‚ÄĒ B7 (local Euler‚ÄďPoincar√© characteristic).]** For every finite discrete `G_‚Ąö‚āā`-module `M`, the continuous cohomology groups `H‚ĀĪ(G_‚Ąö‚āā, M)` are finite for `i = 0, 1, 2`, and `#H¬Ļ(G_‚Ąö‚āā, M) = #H‚Āį(G_‚Ąö‚āā, M) ¬∑ #H¬≤(G_‚Ąö‚āā, M) ¬∑ 2 ^ v‚āā(#M)`. Equivalently `Ōá := #H‚Āį ¬∑ #H¬≤ / #H¬Ļ = ‚ÄĖ#M‚ÄĖ_{‚Ąö‚āā} = 2 ^ (‚ąív‚āā(#M))`. Citation: **NSW [1], Ch. VII ¬ß7.3, Theorem (7.3.1) (Tate)** (`Ōá(k, A) = ‚ÄĖ#A‚ÄĖ_k`); Serre, *Galois Cohomology*, Ch. II ¬ß5.7 Theorem 5; Milne, *ADT* Thm I.2.8. Paper: ¬ß9.2, eq. (145). 1000126: GQ2.FiniteGroup.coprime_fiber_product.{u_1, u_2, u_3} {A : Type u_1} {B : Type u_2} {C : Type u_3} [Group A] [Group B] [Group C] [Finite A] [Finite B] (f : A ‚Üí* C) (g : B ‚Üí* C) (hf : Function.Surjective ‚áĎf) (hg : Function.Surjective ‚áĎg) (hcop : (Nat.card ‚Ü•f.ker).Coprime (Nat.card ‚Ü•g.ker)) (J : Subgroup (A √ó B)) (hJsub : ‚ąÄ p ‚ąą J, f p.1 = g p.2) (hJA : Function.Surjective fun p => (‚ÜĎp).1) (hJB : Function.Surjective fun p => (‚ÜĎp).2) (p : A √ó B) : f p.1 = g p.2 ‚Üí p ‚ąą J**Lemma 9.1 (coprime-kernel subdirect product).** Let `f : A ‚Ü† C` and `g : B ‚Ü† C` be finite epimorphisms whose kernels have coprime orders. A subgroup `J` of `A √ó B` that lies in the fibre product `{(a,b) | f a = g b}` and projects onto both factors is the *entire* fibre product. Proof via Goursat: `J.goursatFst ‚Č§ ker f` and `J.goursatSnd ‚Č§ ker g`, and Goursat's isomorphism `A/goursatFst ‚ČÉ B/goursatSnd` gives `|goursatFst|¬∑|ker g| = |goursatSnd|¬∑|ker f|`. Coprimality of `|ker f|`, `|ker g|` then forces `ker g ‚Č§ J.goursatSnd`, which is exactly the missing wild direction needed to hit every fibre-product element.

1000059: Subtype.{u} {őĪ : Sort u} (p : őĪ ‚Üí Prop) : Sort (max 1 u)All the elements of a type that satisfy a predicate. `Subtype p`, usually written `{ x : őĪ // p x }` or `{ x // p x }`, contains all elements `x : őĪ` for which `p x` is true. Its constructor is a pair of the value and the proof that it satisfies the predicate. In run-time code, `{ x : őĪ // p x }` is represented identically to `őĪ`. There is a coercion from `{ x : őĪ // p x }` to `őĪ`, so elements of a subtype may be used where the underlying type is expected. Examples: * `{ n : Nat // n % 2 = 0 }` is the type of even numbers. * `{ xs : Array String // xs.size = 5 }` is the type of arrays with five `String`s. * Given `xs : List őĪ`, `List { x : őĪ // x ‚ąą xs }` is the type of lists in which all elements are contained in `xs`. Conventions for notations in identifiers: * The recommended spelling of `{ x // p x }` in identifiers is `subtype`.

1000029: Eq.{u_1} {őĪ : Sort u_1} : őĪ ‚Üí őĪ ‚Üí PropThe equality relation. It has one introduction rule, `Eq.refl`. We use `a = b` as notation for `Eq a b`. A fundamental property of equality is that it is an equivalence relation. ``` variable (őĪ : Type) (a b c d : őĪ) variable (hab : a = b) (hcb : c = b) (hcd : c = d) example : a = d := Eq.trans (Eq.trans hab (Eq.symm hcb)) hcd ``` Equality is much more than an equivalence relation, however. It has the important property that every assertion respects the equivalence, in the sense that we can substitute equal expressions without changing the truth value. That is, given `h1 : a = b` and `h2 : p a`, we can construct a proof for `p b` using substitution: `Eq.subst h1 h2`. Example: ``` example (őĪ : Type) (a b : őĪ) (p : őĪ ‚Üí Prop) (h1 : a = b) (h2 : p a) : p b := Eq.subst h1 h2 example (őĪ : Type) (a b : őĪ) (p : őĪ ‚Üí Prop) (h1 : a = b) (h2 : p a) : p b := h1 ‚Ėł h2 ``` The triangle in the second presentation is a macro built on top of `Eq.subst` and `Eq.symm`, and you can enter it by typing `\t`. For more information: [Equality](https://lean-lang.org/theorem_proving_in_lean4/quantifiers_and_equality.html#equality) Conventions for notations in identifiers: * The recommended spelling of `=` in identifiers is `eq`.

For example, if `G` is a group (with group law written as multiplication) and `A` is an abelian group (with group law written as addition), then to give `A` a `G`-module structure (for example, to use the theory of group cohomology) is to say `[DistribMulAction G A]`. 1000006: Finite.{u_3} (őĪ : Sort u_3) : PropA type is `Finite` if it is in bijective correspondence to some `Fin n`. This is similar to `Fintype`, but `Finite` is a proposition rather than data. A particular benefit to this is that `Finite` instances are definitionally equal to one another (due to proof irrelevance) rather than being merely propositionally equal, and, furthermore, `Finite` instances generally avoid the need for `Decidable` instances. One other notable difference is that `Finite` allows there to be `Finite p` instances for all `p : Prop`, which is not allowed by `Fintype` due to universe constraints. An application of this is that `Finite (x ‚ąą s ‚Üí ő≤ x)` follows from the general instance for pi types, assuming `[‚ąÄ x, Finite (ő≤ x)]`. Implementation note: this is a reason `Finite őĪ` is not defined as `Nonempty (Fintype őĪ)`. Every `Fintype` instance provides a `Finite` instance via `Finite.of_fintype`. Conversely, one can noncomputably create a `Fintype` instance from a `Finite` instance via `Fintype.ofFinite`. In a proof one might write ```lean have := Fintype.ofFinite őĪ ``` to obtain such an instance. Do not write noncomputable `Fintype` instances; instead write `Finite` instances and use this `Fintype.ofFinite` interface. The `Fintype` instances should be relied upon to be computable for evaluation purposes. Theorems should use `Finite` instead of `Fintype`, unless definitions in the theorem statement require `Fintype`. Definitions should prefer `Finite` as well, unless it is important that the definitions are meant to be computable in the reduction or `#eval` sense.
